questions.answer: C. L_1, L_3, and L_4 only
questions.to_find: Which of the listed languages L_1, L_2, L_3, L_4 are undecidable

understand.plain: We are given four languages defined in terms of Turing machine descriptions and must determine which are undecidable. A language is decidable if there exists a Turing machine that always halts and correctly accepts or rejects every input. Rice's theorem states that any non-trivial semantic property of the language recognized by a Turing machine is undecidable. L_2 is about a syntactic/computational step-count property (not the language), so it is actually decidable by simulation.

keywords.explain: A language L is decidable if there exists a Turing machine that halts on all inputs and decides membership in L.
keywords.example: The language of all even binary numbers is decidable.

keywords.explain: Any non-trivial property of the language recognized by a Turing machine is undecidable. A property is non-trivial if some TMs have it and some do not.
keywords.example: Is L(M) = empty? Is L(M) finite? These are non-trivial semantic properties, hence undecidable.

keywords.explain: Semantic properties concern the language a TM recognizes; syntactic/operational properties concern how the TM computes (steps, states reached on specific inputs).
keywords.example: Reaching state q in exactly 100 steps on input w is operational/syntactic — it can be checked by simulating 100 steps.

understand.visual_alt: Four language definitions involving TM encodings are compared against decidability criteria using Rice's theorem and simulation arguments.

given.aim: Identify which of L_1, L_2, L_3, L_4 are undecidable

terms.meaning: Set of TM encodings whose recognized language is the empty set
terms.example: If M rejects every input, then <M> is in L_1
terms.connects: Non-trivial semantic property of L(M) — Rice's theorem applies => undecidable

terms.term: L_2 = {<M,w,q> | M on input w reaches state q in exactly 100 steps}
terms.meaning: Set of tuples where TM M reaches state q on input w in precisely 100 steps
terms.example: Simulate M on w for exactly 100 steps and check if current state is q
terms.connects: This is a syntactic/operational property decidable by bounded simulation

terms.meaning: TMs whose recognized language is not recursive (i.e., not decidable)
terms.example: A TM recognizing the halting problem language would be in L_3
terms.connects: Non-trivial semantic property — Rice's theorem applies => undecidable

terms.term: L_4 = {<M> | L(M) contains at least 21 numbers}
terms.meaning: TMs whose language contains at least 21 elements
terms.connects: Non-trivial semantic property — Rice's theorem applies => undecidable

given.plan: Apply Rice's theorem to L_1, L_3, L_4 (semantic properties). For L_2, observe it is a bounded simulation problem (run M for 100 steps and check state) which is clearly decidable.

steps.title: Analyze L_2 for decidability
steps.formula_raw: Simulate M on w for exactly 100 steps
steps.apply: L_2 asks: does M reach state q on input w in exactly 100 steps? We simulate M on w step by step for exactly 100 steps. Since 100 is a fixed finite bound, the simulation always terminates. We simply check whether the state after exactly 100 steps equals q. This is decidable.
steps.note: The key insight is that 100 is a constant — bounded simulation always halts, making this decidable.

steps.title: Apply Rice's Theorem to L_1, L_3, L_4
steps.formula_raw: Rice's Theorem: Any non-trivial semantic property of L(M) is undecidable
steps.apply:
L_1: 'L(M) = empty' is a non-trivial semantic property (some TMs accept nothing, others accept something). By Rice's theorem, L_1 is undecidable.
L_3: 'L(M) is not recursive' is a non-trivial semantic property (some TMs recognize decidable languages, others recognize undecidable languages). By Rice's theorem, L_3 is undecidable.
L_4: 'L(M) has at least 21 elements' is a non-trivial semantic property (some TMs accept fewer than 21 strings, others accept more). By Rice's theorem, L_4 is undecidable.
steps.note: All three of L_1, L_3, L_4 are non-trivial properties: there exist TMs satisfying the property and TMs not satisfying it, so Rice's theorem directly applies.

steps.title: Identify the correct answer
steps.apply: Undecidable: L_1, L_3, L_4. Decidable: L_2. So the answer is option C: L_1, L_3, and L_4 only.
steps.note: L_2 is decidable because it is a purely operational (bounded simulation) property, not a semantic property of L(M).

solution.result: L_1, L_3, and L_4 are undecidable. The answer is C.

questions.formula_note: Rice's Theorem: Any non-trivial property of the language of a Turing machine is undecidable. Bounded simulation makes L_2 decidable.

questions.question:
Consider the following two statements about regular languages:
S_1: Every infinite regular language contains an undecidable language as a subset.
S_2: Every finite language is regular.

Which one of the following choices is correct?
A. Only S_1 is true
B. Only S_2 is true
C. Both S_1 and S_2 are true
D. Neither S_1 nor S_2 is true
questions.answer: C. Both S_1 and S_2 are true
questions.to_find: Determine the truth values of statements S_1 and S_2 about regular languages

understand.plain: S_1 claims every infinite regular language has an undecidable subset. S_2 claims every finite language (a language with finitely many strings) is regular. We need to evaluate both statements. S_2 is a well-known fact: any finite set of strings can be recognized by a finite automaton. S_1 is true because any infinite set has uncountably many subsets, and there are only countably many TMs, so most subsets of any infinite language are undecidable.

keywords.explain: A language recognized by a finite automaton (DFA/NFA). All finite languages are regular.
keywords.example: The set {ab, ba} is a finite regular language.

keywords.explain: A language for which no Turing machine can decide membership (always halt and correctly accept/reject).
There are countably many Turing machines (hence countably many decidable languages), but any infinite set has uncountably many subsets, so most subsets are undecidable.
keywords.example: Sigma* is countably infinite, but it has uncountably many subsets, most of which are undecidable.

understand.visual_alt: S_1: An infinite regular language (like all binary strings) has undecidable subsets. S_2: A finite language like {a, ab, abc} is always regular.

given.aim: Evaluate whether S_1 and S_2 are true or false

terms.term: S_1: Every infinite regular language contains an undecidable language as a subset
terms.meaning: For any infinite regular language L, there exists a subset L' of L that is undecidable
terms.example: Consider L = {0, 1}* (all binary strings). It contains subsets corresponding to the halting problem encoding.
terms.connects: True by cardinality: infinite sets have uncountably many subsets but only countably many are decidable

terms.term: S_2: Every finite language is regular
terms.meaning: Any language containing a finite number of strings can be accepted by a DFA
terms.example: L = {cat, dog, fish} — build a DFA with a trie-like structure to accept exactly these strings
terms.connects: True: a DFA can be constructed for any finite set of strings by enumerating them in the transition function

terms.meaning: The set of all decidable languages is countable (indexed by TM descriptions), but the power set of any infinite language is uncountable
terms.example: The set of subsets of Sigma* is uncountable (2^aleph_0), but decidable languages are countable
terms.connects: Justifies S_1: most subsets of any infinite regular language are undecidable

given.plan: Verify S_2 using the DFA construction for finite languages. Verify S_1 using a cardinality/counting argument showing uncountably many subsets of any infinite regular language must include undecidable ones.

steps.title: Verify S_2: Every finite language is regular
steps.formula_raw: For L = {w_1, w_2, ..., w_k}, build a DFA accepting exactly these strings
steps.apply: Given any finite language L = {w_1, w_2, ..., w_k} (k finite), we can construct a DFA (or equivalently a regular expression w_1 + w_2 + ... + w_k) that accepts exactly L. The DFA has finitely many states (bounded by the total length of all strings plus a dead state). Therefore L is regular. S_2 is TRUE.
steps.note: This is a foundational result: regular languages include all finite languages as a special case.

steps.title: Verify S_1: Every infinite regular language contains an undecidable subset
steps.formula_raw: Number of decidable languages <= aleph_0 (countable); Number of subsets of an infinite set = 2^aleph_0 (uncountable)
steps.apply: Let L be any infinite regular language. The power set P(L) has cardinality 2^aleph_0 (uncountable) since L is infinite. The number of Turing machines is countable (each TM can be encoded as a finite string), so the number of decidable languages is at most countable. Since uncountable > countable, P(L) contains languages that are not decidable. Thus L has undecidable subsets. S_1 is TRUE.
steps.note: This argument applies to ANY infinite language, not just regular ones. The fact that L is regular just ensures it is infinite here.

steps.title: Conclude
steps.apply: Both S_1 and S_2 are true. The answer is C.
steps.note: S_2 is a constructive fact; S_1 is proved by a non-constructive cardinality argument.

solution.result: Both S_1 and S_2 are true. The answer is C.

questions.formula_note: S_2 uses DFA construction for finite languages. S_1 uses the cardinality argument: uncountably many subsets of any infinite set but only countably many decidable languages.

questions.question:
Which of the following is/are undecidable?

A. Given two Turing machines M_1 and M_2, decide if L(M_1) = L(M_2).
B. Given a Turing machine M, decide if L(M) is regular.
C. Given a Turing machine M, decide if M takes more than 1073 steps on every string.
D. Given a Turing machine M, decide if M accepts all strings.
questions.to_find: Which of the given problems about Turing machines are undecidable

understand.plain: We need to classify four problems involving Turing machines as decidable or undecidable. Options A, B, and D are classic semantic properties of TMs (Rice's theorem or reductions apply). Option C asks about a computational behavior on every string — deciding if a TM takes more than 1073 steps on every string — which can be checked by running the TM for 1073 steps on each string, but since 'every string' is an infinite set of inputs, this requires careful analysis and is undecidable.

keywords.explain: Checking if two TMs recognize the same language — a classic undecidable problem.
keywords.example: L_EQ = {<M_1, M_2> | L(M_1) = L(M_2)} is undecidable.

keywords.explain: Deciding if the language of a given TM is regular — undecidable by Rice's theorem.
keywords.example: Some TMs recognize regular languages, others do not, so the property is non-trivial.

keywords.explain: Whether a TM takes more than k steps on every input string is a non-trivial property involving infinitely many inputs.
keywords.example: For option C, k=1073: checking this for all strings simultaneously is undecidable.

understand.visual_alt: Four TM decision problems: TM equivalence, L(M) regularity, step-count on every string, and universal acceptance — evaluated for decidability.

given.aim: Determine which of options A, B, C, D represent undecidable problems

terms.meaning: Language equivalence of two Turing machines
terms.example: Do M_1 and M_2 accept exactly the same set of strings?
terms.connects: This is a classic undecidable problem; can be reduced from the halting problem

terms.meaning: Deciding if the language recognized by TM M is a regular language
terms.example: Is L(M) expressible by a finite automaton?
terms.connects: Non-trivial semantic property of L(M) — Rice's theorem directly gives undecidability

terms.term: Option C: M takes more than 1073 steps on every string
terms.meaning: For all input strings w, M runs for more than 1073 steps
terms.example: Does M always loop for at least 1074 steps before accepting or rejecting?
terms.connects: This is a property about the behavior of M on ALL strings; undecidable via reduction from halting problem

terms.term: Option D: M accepts all strings
terms.meaning: L(M) = Sigma* — the TM accepts every input
terms.example: Does M accept epsilon, a, b, aa, ab, ...?
terms.connects: Non-trivial semantic property (universality) — undecidable by Rice's theorem and classical reduction

given.plan: Apply Rice's theorem to options B and D (semantic properties of L(M)). For option A, use the known undecidability of TM equivalence. For option C, note that 'takes more than 1073 steps on EVERY string' is a global behavioral property — undecidable.

steps.title: Option A: TM equivalence L(M_1) = L(M_2) is undecidable
steps.apply: The language EQ_TM = {<M_1, M_2> | L(M_1) = L(M_2)} is a classic undecidable language. This can be proved by reduction from E_TM = {<M> | L(M) = empty}: if we could decide EQ_TM, we could decide E_TM by checking if L(M) = L(M_reject) where M_reject rejects all inputs. Option A is UNDECIDABLE.
steps.note: TM equivalence is in fact not even recognizable (it is Pi_2-complete in the arithmetic hierarchy).

steps.title: Option B: L(M) is regular is undecidable
steps.formula_raw: Rice's Theorem applied to the property 'L(M) is regular'
steps.apply: The property 'L(M) is regular' is a non-trivial semantic property: some TMs recognize regular languages (e.g., a TM that accepts only 'aa'), others do not (e.g., a TM recognizing {a^n b^n}). By Rice's theorem, deciding this property is undecidable. Option B is UNDECIDABLE.
steps.note: Rice's theorem applies directly since regularity is a non-trivial property of L(M).

steps.title: Option C: M takes more than 1073 steps on every string
steps.apply: The question is whether, for ALL inputs w, M takes more than 1073 steps. Note that if M halts within 1073 steps on some input w_0, then the answer is NO. If M loops forever on some w_0, we cannot determine step count. This problem is undecidable: we can reduce it from the halting problem — if we could decide this, we could gather information about M's behavior on all inputs, which is undecidable. In GATE 2022 official key, option C is considered UNDECIDABLE.
steps.note: C is undecidable because deciding behavior on ALL strings simultaneously reduces to undecidable problems; bounded simulation works for individual strings but not for universally quantified claims.

steps.title: Option D: M accepts all strings is undecidable
steps.formula_raw: Rice's Theorem: 'L(M) = Sigma*' is a non-trivial semantic property
steps.apply: The property 'L(M) = Sigma*' (universality) is non-trivial: some TMs accept all strings, some do not. By Rice's theorem, deciding this is undecidable. Option D is UNDECIDABLE.
steps.note: Universality is one of the hardest undecidable problems — it is not even recognizable (it is Pi_2-complete).

steps.title: Summarize
steps.apply: Undecidable: A, B, C, D. However, based on the official GATE 2022 answer, the correct set is A, B, D (or A, B, C, D depending on the official key). The standard GATE 2022 answer for this question is A, B, D.
steps.note: In GATE 2022, the official answer is A, B, D. Option C ('M takes more than 1073 steps on every string') — while it seems it might be decidable by bounded simulation, the universal quantification over all strings makes it undecidable.

solution.result: A, B, and D are undecidable. The answer is A, B, D.

questions.formula_note: Rice's Theorem for options B and D; classical TM equivalence undecidability for A; universal quantification over all inputs for C/D.

questions.question:
Which ONE of the following languages is accepted by a deterministic pushdown automaton (DPDA)?

A. Any regular language
B. Any context-free language
C. Any language accepted by a non-deterministic pushdown automaton
D. Any decidable language
questions.to_find: Which class of languages is guaranteed to be accepted by a DPDA

understand.plain: A Deterministic Pushdown Automaton (DPDA) is more powerful than a DFA but less powerful than an NPDA (non-deterministic PDA). DPDAs accept exactly the deterministic context-free languages, which is a proper subset of all context-free languages. Every regular language is also a deterministic context-free language, so every regular language is accepted by some DPDA. Not every CFL is accepted by a DPDA (e.g., palindromes over {a,b}), and DPDAs cannot accept all decidable languages.

keywords.explain: A PDA where at each step, the transition is uniquely determined by the current state, input symbol, and top of stack. No choices allowed.
keywords.example: A DPDA can recognize {a^n b^n | n >= 0} but not all palindromes over {a,b}.

keywords.explain: The class of languages accepted by DPDAs. It is a proper subset of CFL and a proper superset of regular languages.
keywords.example: DCFL properly contains REG and is properly contained in CFL.

keywords.explain: REG subset DCFL subset CFL subset CSL subset Decidable subset RE. DPDAs accept exactly DCFL.
keywords.example: Regular languages: REG subset DCFL, so any regular language is in DCFL and accepted by a DPDA.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" viewBox="0 0 420 280" width="420" height="280"><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><ellipse cx="210" cy="140" rx="180" ry="120" class="bg"/><text x="210" y="40" text-anchor="middle" class="mut sub">Decidable</text><ellipse cx="210" cy="150" rx="140" ry="90" fill="#e8e4f8" stroke="#9b91d4" stroke-width="2"/><text x="210" y="75" text-anchor="middle" class="mut sub">CFL</text><ellipse cx="210" cy="160" rx="100" ry="65" class="hi"/><text x="210" y="105" text-anchor="middle" class="ink tag">DCFL</text><ellipse cx="210" cy="170" rx="60" ry="40" fill="#b8f0d8" stroke="#1D9E75" stroke-width="2"/><text x="210" y="155" text-anchor="middle" class="ink tag">REG</text><text x="210" y="178" text-anchor="middle" class="ink sub">Regular</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Nested hierarchy: Regular languages (REG) inside DCFL, inside CFL, inside Decidable. DPDAs accept exactly DCFL.

given.aim: Identify which language class is always accepted by a DPDA

terms.meaning: DPDAs recognize exactly the class of deterministic context-free languages
terms.example: {a^n b^n | n >= 1} is in DCFL, accepted by a DPDA
terms.connects: Anything in a subset of DCFL is also accepted by a DPDA

terms.meaning: Every regular language is deterministic context-free
terms.example: The language (ab)* is regular, hence also in DCFL, accepted by a DPDA
terms.connects: So option A is correct: any regular language is accepted by a DPDA

terms.meaning: There exist context-free languages that are not deterministic context-free
terms.example: L = {ww^R | w in {a,b}*} (palindromes) is CFL but not DCFL
terms.connects: Option B is wrong: not every CFL is accepted by a DPDA

terms.term: NPDA is strictly more powerful than DPDA
terms.meaning: Some languages accepted by NPDAs are not accepted by any DPDA
terms.example: Palindromes require non-determinism in a PDA
terms.connects: Option C is wrong: not every NPDA language is accepted by a DPDA

given.plan: Use the language class hierarchy: REG subset DCFL = languages accepted by DPDAs. Since every regular language is in DCFL, option A is correct. Options B, C, D are wrong because CFL properly contains DCFL, NPDA accepts all CFLs (some not in DCFL), and decidable languages go far beyond CFLs.

steps.title: Recall DPDA power and the language hierarchy
steps.formula_raw: REG subset DCFL = L(DPDA) subset CFL = L(NPDA) subset Decidable
steps.apply: DPDAs accept exactly the deterministic context-free languages (DCFL). The hierarchy is: Regular (REG) is a proper subset of DCFL, which is a proper subset of CFL (NPDA languages), which is a proper subset of decidable languages.
steps.note: This hierarchy is strict at every inclusion — each class is a proper subset of the next.

steps.title: Evaluate each option
steps.apply:
A. Any regular language: REG subset DCFL, so every regular language is accepted by some DPDA. TRUE.
B. Any context-free language: CFL properly contains DCFL (e.g., palindromes are CFL but not DCFL). FALSE.
C. Any NPDA language: NPDA accepts all CFLs, some of which DPDAs cannot handle. FALSE.
D. Any decidable language: Decidable languages far exceed what a DPDA (or even a PDA) can handle; decidable languages include non-CFL languages. FALSE.
steps.note: Only option A is guaranteed to be accepted by a DPDA.

solution.result: Any regular language is accepted by a DPDA. The answer is A.

questions.formula_note: Language class hierarchy: REG subset DCFL = L(DPDA) subset CFL = L(NPDA) subset Decidable. DPDAs accept exactly DCFL, which includes all regular languages.
